parsing_res_list.block_content: 4D WEDNESDAY JANUARY 8,2025 USA TOday E2
STAGE
Patrick RyanUSA TODAY

G 
mmim
Severed noggins, staircase tumbles and gaping stomach wounds.

With all the onstage magic happening in"Death Becomes He," it's enough to make your head spin. Adapted from the1992 camp classic starring Meryl Seep d e  e v Broadway musical serves up irreverent comedy with dumbfounding feats of theatrical sorcery.

The show tells the story of caustic     )and Helen (ennifer Simard), who ingest a purpe poion fom a merios ocialite lle li) th is them el ht te ntive elixir has unsavory side effects:Even the slightest knock to their va-vavoom figures causes irreparable blemishes. So when the gloves come off and the ladies brawl, all manner of absurd body horror ensues.

公司a The movie won an Oscar for its outrageous visual effects. So naturally, illuionist Tim Clother was daund w ne got the call about helping bring "Death Becomes Her" to the New York stage (taking the mantle from ob a,who oversaw the ef ects for the show's Chicago tryout early last year).

T Gosh,there's going to be some very ricky bits to work out" says Clothier,whose past credits include Cirque du Soleil and Blue Man Group."It's not a spectacle in Vegas where the magic akes center stage - here, the magic es t ie  y   ly to pull from our box of trics.From a hisorical perspective, there are eferences o classic things that magicians would normally do, as well as some groundbreaking things wedeveloped that no ne's really tried before."
Death Becomes Her' pulls off a aw-dropping staircase scene

The show's action centerpiece, if you will, is the Act 1 fi nale. In a heated conrontationwith Helen and Ernest (Christopher Sieber), the shlubby object ofboth their affections,Madelines busheddown a mansionstaircase,crunching her bones and mangling her imbs all the way down.
The slow-motion plunge is one of the movie'siconicimages, anddrector/horeographer Christopher Gattelli "Newsies") racked his brain for months bout how to bring that to life on stage.
"We literally tried everything: pupes, throwing dolls downstairs, exeriseballs with wigs on them. We hought maybe we could build a robot,"
Gattelli recalls with a laugh. "Nothing felt right."：
Tentg But he eventually cracked the code when he met actor/dancer Warren Yang,an Olympic-trained gymnast who now serves as the production's acrobatics captain.ca

captares "I was on Instagram and I saw a video of Warren literally changing his clothes while he was doing a handstand," Gattelli says. "I was like,'Wait a minute' I reached out to him and was like, 'I can see a world where there's a pommel horse routine down a flight of stairs.'And he was like, 'Oh, I'm all in – let's play'

Pray.Gattelli calls the sequence a"true collaborative ef ort," working with lighting designer Justin Townsend ("Moulin Rouge! The Musical") and costume designer Paul Tazewell ("Wicked") to cleverly achieve the illusion that it's actually Hilty and not Yang toppling down the  stairs. He also had to find other dancers with that particular skill set, should Yang ever need to call out of a perfor–mance.

110000"It's an epic moment that everyone is waiting for and I was just thrilled where it landed," Gattelli says. "Warren stops the show every night with it. There are no wires - he's literally doing these handstands and cartwheels and flips.It's a total free fall and I just cannot believe he can do that."
The show's neck-twisting illusions are old-school 'theatrical magic'

From there, the musical's optical illusions only get wilder: Madeline has her head knocked of during a shovel-wielding showdown, with the help of stunt doubles and props including an eyetricking serving cart. She also blows a hole through Helen's stomach with a shotgun. Of course, unlike the movie,audiences cannot see directly through Simard's torso, which instead billows smoke throughout Act 2.a
"I can't share the secrets because then I would have to kill you," Clothier says slyly. "But there were many different iterations of that throughout development."

Po The moment, too, when an arrow fl ies through Helen's belly is based on a "classic magic eff ect from the 1890s. We had to fi nd visual ways to tell the story of someone who's had a hole blown through their stomach, and that was one of those."
[a

full 360 is also "just good old-fashioned theatrical magic," Gattelli adds. "If you really look at it, it's not hard to tell how it happens. But if the audience is on the ride, it's OK if you see a little bit of how the sausage is made. It's happening live right in front of you, and it's satisfying that the audience eats it up the way they do."

E Gattelli is quick to note that it takes "dozens of people" to pull of these illusions eight shows a week. An hour before each performance, various cast and crew members are behind the curtain running through each and every stunt to ensure they're safe.

[Ta Gattelli says. "It really frees everyone up, so when they do the show, they can feel confi dent and breathe and be funny.It helps them let loose and give those amazing performances – and that's always the goal."
"Death Becomes Her" is now playing at the Lunt-Fontanne Theatre (205 W 46th St.).
TVPREVIEW
Fox'sDoc' is a drama to forget
Kelly Lawler USA TODAY
Not long into watching Fox's new ami ""      ff

There are certainly worse TV series han “Doc”(Tuesdays, 9 EST/PST,★☆), which llows Dr.Amy Larsen Molly Parker), a callous medical proessional who loses eight years of her nemory aumic bain ijury.here are series with lazy writing,  characrs,bin  and een ff ensive elements. But the sin of this   .司添
Ner.eathelids "Doc"is,as thekids might say,cringe." It's awkwad, le a ritating.tiitily epress, ad t u c ontertainment."n
In Kingman("Magnum,PI"),Parkerand he rest of the cast,"Doc" never colesces into a coherent and functional V show.Itfliom its frst few noments.a

Amy is every stereotype TV has ever leveloped about an unfeeling doctor ho is brilliant but lacks bedside man r.er e gi er, but she gets results, gosh darn i.he's chief of internal medicine at a Minneapolis hospital where she annoys r ex-husband and hospital admir icl r l me Miller (Scott Wolf) and makes time for liscreet rendezvous with young hunk ake El -Ml Eck).

vhen Amyis in a car accident that avs hrh  m  u Vhen she wakes up from surgery she hinks that it's 2016 and that she's mariedto Michael. She also thinksher laughter is 9 and her son is alive, and he is hit with major grief to learn her aughter is 17 and her son died seven
Molly Parker stars as Dr. Amy Larsen in "Doc." PROVIDED BY PETER H. STRANKS/FOX
years ago. Oh, and she's a much nicer person without those eight years, too.

ries, has potential. But "Doc" never does anything interesting with the plot. It's all reduced to simplistic binaries. Amnesia Amy is good, present-day Amy is bad. Amy is a medical genius and the rest of the doctors are pale imitations.Amy loves her ex-husband, who has moved on. Amy's new fl ing can't tell her they were together because of offi ce politics, It's all rather tedious.

司and not in the good way. Amy's every interaction is loaded with dramatic irony,but the audience isn't in a good spot knowing that her teenage daughter actually hates her or that the new chief of internal medicine is a quack who killed a patient. We're just kind of stressed out by it.公司
It's a shame, because Parker is a firstrate actress with great credits including "Deadwood" and "House of Cards." She is almost enough of a talent to build this aa
The series can't decide if it cares more about Amy's amnesia and personal relationships or the case-of-theweek medical drama.aaa
Each episode is heavily unbalaned and poorly paced.a
ana pooryPa much work. I'd rather forget all about it.

Adapted from the1992 camp classic starring Meryl Seep d e e v Broadway musical serves up irreverent comedy with dumbfounding feats of theatrical sorcery. So when the gloves come off and the ladies brawl, all manner of absurd body horror ensues. 公司a The movie won an Oscar for its outrageous visual effects. So naturally, illuionist Tim Clother was daund w ne got the call about helping bring "Death Becomes Her" to the New York stage (taking the mantle from ob a,who oversaw the ef ects for the show's Chicago tryout early last year). T Gosh,there's going to be some very ricky bits to work out" says Clothier,whose past credits include Cirque du Soleil and Blue Man Group."It's not a spectacle in Vegas where the magic akes center stage - here, the magic es t ie y ly to pull from our box of trics.From a hisorical perspective, there are eferences o classic things that magicians would normally do, as well as some groundbreaking things wedeveloped that no ne's really tried before." ## Death Becomes Her' pulls off a aw-dropping staircase scene The show's action centerpiece, if you will, is the Act 1 fi nale. The Musical") and costume designer Paul Tazewell ("Wicked") to cleverly achieve the illusion that it's actually Hilty and not Yang toppling down the stairs. He also had to find other dancers with that particular skill set, should Yang ever need to call out of a perfor–mance. 110000"It's an epic moment that everyone is waiting for and I was just thrilled where it landed," Gattelli says. "But there were many different iterations of that throughout development."Po The moment, too, when an arrow fl ies through Helen's belly is based on a "classic magic eff ect from the 1890s. We had to fi nd visual ways to tell the story of someone who's had a hole blown through their stomach, and that was one of those." [a full 360 is also "just good old-fashioned theatrical magic," Gattelli adds. "If you really look at it, it's not hard to tell how it happens. But if the audience is on the ride, it's OK if you see a little bit of how the sausage is made. It's happening live right in front of you, and it's satisfying that the audience eats it up the way they do." E Gattelli is quick to note that it takes "dozens of people" to pull of these illusions eight shows a week. An hour before each performance, various cast and crew members are behind the curtain running through each and every stunt to ensure they're safe. [Ta Gattelli says. years ago. Oh, and she's a much nicer person without those eight years, too. ries, has potential. But "Doc" never does anything interesting with the plot. It's all reduced to simplistic binaries. Amnesia Amy is good, present-day Amy is bad. Amy is a medical genius and the rest of the doctors are pale imitations.Amy loves her ex-husband, who has moved on. We're just kind of stressed out by it.公司It's a shame, because Parker is a firstrate actress with great credits including "Deadwood" and "House of Cards." She is almost enough of a talent to build this aa The series can't decide if it cares more about Amy's amnesia and personal relationships or the case-of-theweek medical drama.aaa Each episode is heavily unbalaned and poorly paced.a ana pooryPa much work. I'd rather forget all about it.
